dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond to a suspected Hemorrhage/Lacerations call at --Address Redacted--, Apt 5.
Hi ma'am, I'm Sam, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I have lower back and abdominal pain, heavy vaginal bleeding for 5 days now, and I fell yesterday when I fainted.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name and the date today please?
Good, you look awake. Can you hear me and tell me what date it is today?
And how are you feeling today, if you don't mind me asking?
We're going to help you get into a more comfortable position, if that's okay with you.
Tell me, have you experienced any vomiting, chest pain, or shortness of breath?
No, only some weakness, and this constant heavy bleeding and pain is unusual for me.
Any allergies to medications or foods, or have you ever had an allergic reaction before, ma'am?
No known drug or food allergies, to my knowledge.
What medications are you currently taking today, if any?
Tramadol, some vitamins, like vitamin D, calcium, potassium, and ferrous gluconate. I also take Premarin and a laxative called bisacodyl.
Ma'am, can you tell me about any past surgeries or health problems you've had?

I had a laparoscopic hysterectomy in January of this year and a c-section 40 years ago. I had a UTI in August this year that was treated with an antibiotic. I was told I had an "enlarged heart" when I got cleared for anesthesia before my surgery, but no further treatment was needed.
What's the last thing you ate, and when was the last time you had something to drink or eat something solid recently?
I haven't been eating much in the past five days. I got some food down, but almost didn't touch it today.
Can you describe to me what happened the day of your fall yesterday and the leading events before your call for help?
I stood up this morning and suddenly became weak and fell down. My left shoulder was hurt, and that's when I realized I must have fainted.
Ma'am, how long has this pain been going on for you now? Like since the fall or for how many days?
Has anything you do, such as moving or touching your belly, made the pain better or worse for you, if so please tell me about that.
How does this pain feel? Like sharp, dull, or throbbing in your abdomen and back area?
On a scale, how severe is that pain, with 10 being the worst you've ever had?
It's about 4 usually, a constant ache and twinge.
When did the pain first start, and has it remained steady, gotten worse, or gone up and down?
You mentioned heavy vaginal bleeding and lower abdominal pain. Anything else about your digestion, like more constipation or bowel movement changes recently?
Yes, I've been constipated over the past week. I need to take laxatives to go but haven't had a bowel movement.
Please be quiet while I gently press my fingers into your abdomen for tenderness. Left and right lower quadrants are reactive to touch.
Respirations are 18, regular. Pulse oximeter shows 98%.
Pulse is 106 and strong. Skin is dry. Blood pressure is 125 over 79.
We're just going to do a general review of your skin tone and temperature.
Skin is pink and warm, not diaphoretic or clammy.
You're wearing some adult incontinence pull-ups. Is it okay if we note that for the record?
Yes. The bed had old blood on it from either falling or maybe a regular issue due to her aging.
Pulse is 106, strong and regular.
Respirations are 18, regular.
Blood pressure is 125 over 79.
Oxygen saturation is 98% on room air. Normal findings.
Blood glucose is not currently obtainable right now.
4 lead is attached and rhythm shows sinus with some PVCs.
Let's try an IV in the left arm. I'm going to check for a vein.
One IV attempt in the left AC vein, unsuccessful. We'll keep her in a position of comfort for now.
Based on patient's presentation, vitals, and EKG, I'm going to follow protocol for medical abdominal pain.
Vital signs remain stable, with heart rate slightly lower from 106 to 103 and blood pressure now at 134 over 81.
We took another 12-lead and still see the STEMI alerts on the monitor.
Let me recheck your pain, just to make sure. Severity on a scale?
Still about 4. It hasn't gotten worse or better, and the bleeding continues.
We need to head to VCU for specialized care, especially with the STEMI noted on our EKG. That's the best option for her right now.
EMS is moving the patient to the ambulance using a stair chair and onto a stretcher. She's too weak to walk and must be placed with sheet assist.
Ma'am, during the ride, is there anything you've noticed about the pain or bleeding different from when we first met?
I haven't felt anything change. Just sore in the shoulder and tired.
We called VCU ahead with a report of her medical conditions, medications, and multiple EKG findings including STEMI.
History, vitals, EKG results, and current findings are provided for transport. The patient is transported in a position of comfort.
Ma'am, we need to get to Virginia Commonwealth University Hospital quickly. Will you allow us to transport you there to get further care? We'll do everything we can to make it a smooth ride for you.
Yes, take me there then. I'd like to get better. I'll hold on as long as I can.
Insurance noted: Medicare and Medicaid. All medications, EKG, and vitals are captured and ready for medical handoff.
Transport has started to VCU Health Systems now.
Yes, medical is waiting with a team for transfer and has dispatched a nurse and doctor for further evaluations. Thanks for the update.
You're welcome. We'll be there in ten minutes. VCU, Unit 3 is going to transport for further evaluations.
Ma'am, we're at VCU's hospital now. Let's help get you to the ER as gently as we can. You're with us until they take over care now.
EMS has unloaded the patient with three healthcare workers using a sheet assist. All vitals are stable and patient is alert.
Transfer to the VCU ED has been documented and complete. Medical reports are handed off; all vitals, interventions, and HPI are done.
Final vitals are accomplished before transfer as well as a full medical history and secondary assessment. All abnormalities noted.
Patient is alert, pain remains at 4, O2 saturation 96%, BP 134/81, HR 103. Moving with assistance ahead now.
Our role here is done. The doctors and nurses at VCU will take over next. We're glad you're in good hands now, ma'am.
